title: GDPR Unauthorized Cloud Service Detection (Shadow SaaS)
exclusions: Approved SaaS evaluations in the IT evaluation register during documented trial periods; personal cloud access from corporate networks not involving corporate data upload.
evidence: Shadow SaaS detection report showing discovered unapproved services, risk classification, data processing assessment, and remediation actions (approve, block, or migrate).

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.5(2) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.5(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.41: GDPR Unauthorized Cloud Service Detection (Shadow SaaS).
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.41' running on index=proxy, DNS logs, ms:o365:management (OAuth consent), gdpr_approved_saas_catalog.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.32 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.32 (Security of processing) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.41: GDPR Unauthorized Cloud Service Detection (Shadow SaaS).
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.41' running on index=proxy, DNS logs, ms:o365:management (OAuth consent), gdpr_approved_saas_catalog.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Generate test DNS queries and proxy logs showing access to an unregistered SaaS application (e.g., unknown-saas.example.com) from corporate endpoints; verify the shadow SaaS detection identifies the unapproved service.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Generate test traffic to registered and approved SaaS applications in the corporate service catalogue; verify no shadow SaaS alert fires for approved services.

app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (Splunkbase 4055), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263)
spl:
index=proxy earliest=-24h
| stats sum(bytes_out) as upload_bytes dc(user) as users by dest_host
| lookup gdpr_approved_saas_catalog.csv saas_domain AS dest_host OUTPUT approval_status
| where isnull(approval_status) OR approval_status!="approved"
| where upload_bytes > 10485760
| sort - upload_bytes
| table dest_host, upload_bytes, users, approval_status
description: Personal data processed in unapproved SaaS breaks accountability and security measures. This use case identifies cloud applications from proxy, DNS, and CASB logs that are not in the approved catalogue — prioritising services with upload behaviour and OAuth grants to corporate identities.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes proxy. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain approved SaaS domain list with data residency notes; (2) baseline proxy categories; (3) alert on high upload to unknown SaaS; (4) correlate with O365 OAuth events for enterprise SSO abuse; (5) feed discoveries into ROPA gap analysis and enterprise architecture review. Indexes required: `index=proxy`; Lookups: `gdpr_approved_saas_catalog`, `gdpr_approved_saas_catalog.csv`.
visualization: Table (unapproved SaaS), Bar chart (upload volume), Word cloud (domains — optional), Single value (new domains this week). Key fields: `approval_status`, `dest_host`, `upload_bytes`, `users`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Sanctioned SaaS evaluation periods** — IT teams evaluating new SaaS solutions during approved trial periods may generate cloud access patterns before formal approval; check the IT evaluation register.

2. **BYOD personal cloud usage** — Employee personal cloud storage access from corporate networks that does not involve corporate data may be flagged as shadow IT; verify whether corporate data was actually uploaded.

3. **Marketing technology tools** — Marketing teams using approved category-level authorisations for campaign tools may show as unapproved individual tools within an approved category.

4. **API integration middleware** — Approved applications connecting through third-party integration platforms (Zapier, MuleSoft) may show the middleware as an unapproved service.

5. **Browser-based utilities** — Non-data-processing web utilities (calculators, converters) accessed from corporate browsers do not constitute shadow SaaS processing personal data.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `proxy` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `gdpr_approved_saas_catalog.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('gdpr_approved_saas_catalog.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.5(2)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.5(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.41: GDPR Unauthorized Cloud Service Detection (Shadow SaaS)." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `proxy` under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Maintain approved SaaS domain list with data residency notes. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** baseline proxy categories. The register referenced here is primary evidence — treat changes to it with the same discipline as code changes.

• **Hint 3:** alert on high upload to unknown SaaS. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** correlate with O365 OAuth events for enterprise SSO abuse. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** feed discoveries into ROPA gap analysis and enterprise architecture review. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR Unauthorized Cloud Service Detection (Shadow SaaS)," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the GDPR control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `gdpr_approved_saas_catalog.csv` — the exception register containing approved, time-bound deviations from policy. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`approval_status`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `saas_domain`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `gdpr_approved_saas_catalog.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval saas_domain = lower(saas_domain)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 24 hours.

Numeric threshold `upload_bytes > 10485760`: this is the noise-to-signal decision point for the detection. Calibrate against your estate's baseline — see the 'Troubleshooting' section below for the calibration query.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.41: GDPR Unauthorized Cloud Service Detection (Shadow SaaS)]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.41,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=proxy sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `approval_status`, `dest_host`, `upload_bytes`, `users`. Run:

```spl
index=proxy sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table approval_status, dest_host, upload_bytes, users
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `gdpr_approved_saas_catalog.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup gdpr_approved_saas_catalog.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `upload_bytes`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(upload_bytes) as p50 perc90(upload_bytes) as p90 perc99(upload_bytes) as p99 max(upload_bytes) as hi
```

The current threshold `10485760` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.41: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`unapproved SaaS`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Distribution (`upload volume`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 3 — Word Cloud (`domains — optional`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 4 — Single-value KPI (`new domains this week`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We personal data processed in unapproved SaaS breaks accountability and security measures.
